speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three. Today is May 31, 1996, and the survivor is Sol Glick. So you-- you rubbed the dirt into your wound--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and everything healed. Three days later, when she [? looked ?] [? at it, ?] she said, everything is perfect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in this spot, but the other people in the train moved on or they all were-- they-- they all remained there? You had come down and fallen off the ladder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I was already in concentration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were bringing the lunch. It was in the dark. Nine months were in the dark there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I fell down. We were there. And I asked her, why is-- sending me to work back? She said, they're going to give you light work, not the hard work. And I said to her, have pity. She said she was a prisoner herself. Anyway, I went back to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me a light work and everything. Later [INAUDIBLE]. Are they gonna send away some people in another-- in another camp just a couple months later-- was a-- are we gonna send away another camp in this camp prisoners who were there with us, killers? And these were the big-- you cannot run no place. It's the end of the world, Eskimos live there. Anyway, and they say, there you finish your life. There they-- they get you out. All of a sudden I came home from work, three Russian standing soldiers and they take me. Go in and get your clothes and come with us. And I said to him in Russian, where are you taking me? Are you taking me to-- he would not talk to me. We were walking for a half an hour and we got to a place and I said, oh my God. Thousands of soldiers, Polish [INAUDIBLE]. And they say, you go and be free. Started to go to the camp. We're gonna be free. I said, what? We're gonna be free. I never believed so many people are there. When I was in my camp, I don't know, I ran all over there with all the soldiers, hundreds of thousands-- Polacks. Not just Poles-- Jews, everything from Poland. Anyway, but one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: My best friend-- when they ask you your life's story, when they ask you where you were born, where you-- everything, I always tell the truth. And this guys, when he was arrested, he tell he was born in Germany. Germany was their allies with-- with-- with Russia. You see, they attacked Poland. And he said he's a German instead of a Polack. When we were free, he was not called to be free. He's a German. When Germany attacked the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Russia. It was no good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When Germany attacked Russia, we don't know about it. And we-- we were free. America supplied with the food. And we were sitting there for a week, till they-- they get the papers to see if you're the right person and everything. They give you-- and this guy was crying, I lied. I'm from Poland. I'm this. I don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't help.

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us money, and they give us food, and we were going to the army from the Polish Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to go into the Polish-- no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. When Hitler attacked Russia, the Polish General Sikorski-- General Sikorski flew from London to Moscow. He made a pact with Stalin to leave the-- all the Polish prisoners in the army. And the-- and the-- be allies. America supplied the food, the uniforms and we went from the North Pole to the Far East, the tropical. [INAUDIBLE] was the place, near Tashkent. You hear about Tashkent, Samarkand, Uzbekistan near the Iranian border there climate. They are the Arabs, but they're not the Arabs. The are Uzbeks. They call them Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan. We were there and the tanks was going-- people were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, trains. We got the trains, special trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know, but how long were you on the train to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for a week or more. In every state, they had to bring food and they stopped and they changed the locomotive, the-- the trains. Anyway, we got to Samarkand--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did they serve you there? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the trains we were already American food. We went to bakeries and everything and they would get the bread. We had good food. America supplied the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had been-- you had been up in the North Pole for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a year and half.

speaker: interviewer
text: About a year and a half. So now it's about 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: When Hitler attacked-- when Hitler attacked Russia, we were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is, what, 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: '40-- '42. Close to '42, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: About '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway-- and I know my brothers was Avraham [INAUDIBLE]. As a city-- the family, they arrest him and they threw him with his wife and children and my brother, who-- who was with them. As a family, they put them together and they sent him there to work. He was not onto the-- by [INAUDIBLE] the guns. And [INAUDIBLE] could not move there. And they released all the prisoners from-- all the people from there, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time we stopped and I said, let me see-- my brother had two children. And I see-- and I-- we-- we had a couple of hours walk around in the station. And I went-- right there by the station was orphans for children. And I walked over to them, Polish children, and I said, you know my-- my-- my nieces were here and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know nothing. My brother was every day by this station, my older brother. And he know--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My older brother, he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Avraham you said went off to Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Zanwel, ?] he was here with his wife and children and their two brothers, when they were released, they lived there. And I walked to the station. I walk and I-- and I-- and I-- and-- and [INAUDIBLE] for to supply the food to the army, everything. And my brother oldest was every day looking for me. And the moment that my train came in, he went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I meet a guy and I said, are you-- was in camp? He said, yes. Where? [INAUDIBLE]. I said, you was with my brother? He said, what's your brother? I said, Galek. Oh, yes. I was with him. Your brother was here. Just now he went home. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to go with the army. And I told him, look, my brothers lived in [INAUDIBLE]. I want to see him. I want to make a [? decision. ?] And-- and the-- the army, they know me. I was playing soccer. They know me. And they said to me, look, where are you going? Leave Russia. Don't worry about it. We are going-- soon we get to the army, we go into Iran and everything. I said, no. I want to see my brother. They gave me a pass to get a train and go there for two weeks. And if you decide to stay there, we cannot force you. You stay there. I came back and I-- and my brother was waiting in a train there. I came back and he took me to his house. He walked into his wife and said, I got-- I guessed. She didn't know. She-- she was born in the same city, went to school with me. Oh, she started to scream. And my brother said, you're not going back. You stay with us. Thank God [INAUDIBLE]. I save my brother's life and the children. And-- and I got a job with their work. I went back and I told them, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got a job where?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my brother's work. In [INAUDIBLE] supplying food to the army and everything. My brother was afraid for anything. He worked in a meat-- he was a-- he was a butcher at home. He worked by cutting meat. And there in Russia, you cannot live. You have to steal anything. But when you steal something, they arrest you and they put you in jail. And I got contact with the police right away. I got contact with the police and with the judges and everything. And I said, you want to make money, to the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had nerve, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- look, two people together. He can do nothing. He said, yes. And later on I-- I had a girlfriend there, I was going out and I-- and the judge, we were going out dancing and everything. And I used to take out whole cows. I took it out. The guy who stayed in the factory, he-- he was paid, too-- everything. Later on, I was working in the meat in the black market. [INAUDIBLE] people came to the house and bought the meat and everything from black market. And I took the money and I split it with the judge, with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Police.

speaker: interviewee
text: --police with the guy who was the guard and everything. We were [? getting ?] 100 rubles for a pound of meat. I said, I'm gonna quit this. I used to be delivering the-- the-- the food to the army and everything. I'm gonna go to a tea factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: A tea factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A tea-- [INAUDIBLE] the [INAUDIBLE] the tea-- they-- they-- they take tea, the Arabs and everything. And the tea was 1,000 rubles a packet. I said, I'm gonna go there, take a job in the tea factory. I got a job with the tea factory and they would bring in the trains with the tea and I used to unload them, 100 pounds [INAUDIBLE]. There was a wall there and a police around the wall-- nobody should steal anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it was very valuable, the tea.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and I said to the chief of the police, what do-- what do you think? I take 100 pound bag and I throw across the-- the wall. And you stay there. When I get up, I pick it up and we go home and the invalids who had come back from the war with legs, with everything, they were in the black market. They were waiting in the house to buy it. Anyway, soon I got finished work, I go over there--

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked-- so you were working with the guards who were guarding the tea.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards who the police was there waiting for me so nobody should take it. When I finished work, I took the bagged tea on my shoulder, and he was walking with me with a bayonet like he arrest me and nobody should touch me. Instead of going to the police station, he went to my house and there, the-- the people were waiting. My brother didn't know about it. He came to visit me and looked in the window and see police in my house. He give it to me, don't tell them I'm your brother, and I don't want no-- they know me. He was so afraid for the [? shadow. ?] And-- and I-- if not from me, I supply them-- my nieces got the-- sick, the typhoid, they almost died, food. And I did everything for them. When I got there, I-- I supply with meat, with everything to eat. If not, they would die. After the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was working. He was working.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was working, he made a salary, but he would not take anything--

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: --out from the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But-- oh, his money that he made wasn't enough to--

speaker: interviewee
text: He made-- he made for a month's money he could spend for a day on food and [INAUDIBLE]. And I stayed with my brother there till the-- till the-- the war was almost over. And I-- and I went back to Poland. I went back to Poland. The first thing I-- I landed in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. Hold it. What year was that? When you say war was over, war was over in different places at different times. So you're saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, 1944, the end of '44, I went back from Samarkand to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a ticket. I had money. I bought a ticket and I went back to Blonie. And I came back there from the border. I took a train and I went to Warsaw. And the Polacks saw me in Warsaw, they saw a ghost. You're alive? How'd you survive?

speaker: interviewer
text: These-- these were Poles, nobody that you knew. People that you knew or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I know them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh these people, you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my friends. They-- they were thinking I went to the oven with my brother from the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't know I was a prisoner in Russia. And they said, hey, you're alive? I said, sure. And-- and I meant to my town, to Blonie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait-- so those people that you met, they were Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poles, yes. I went to-- to-- to-- to my city, and I-- and I got the news of what they did to the family, what they-- how they took them to the ghetto in Warsaw and how they died and everything. The house where I used to live, was somebody else there. But I was friendly with the priest in Poland, where family was very, very-- I used to-- we are like my friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I'm going to the church and see the priest, talk to him what happened to my family, [? your ?] family. I came in was two Polacks sitting there, two Polacks. And on said, who are you? I said, I was born here, live here. He took out the uniform with the garb as a priest. I said, I want to see the priest. He said, come. I take you to the sanctuary. He's there. I come in there. He went out for another city, the priest. But the woman who worked there. He said-- I called the-- the-- the woman who works in the church, she said, who are you? I said, Galek. My God, I remember you were a little boy. Come in and have a coffee and everything. I said, I'm not hungry. She hugged me and kissed me. And she tells me this story, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later when I went to the police station-- I went to the police station, and I'm waited for them to give me a pass. I want to go a pass back to-- to Krakow. I already--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to go to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow was not destroyed. It was a city. All the people settled there and I had a friend, [INAUDIBLE], who lived in Krakow. And he had apartments. He said, you come back to me, to Krakow, I give you an apartment and put in business. And I'm standing there in the police station waiting to sign my free ticket. I said to them, where is the police chief? He says, here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? He was a [? thief. ?] I-- unbelievable. I was-- I was-- he was no good. He was a [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, anything. He's the police. He said to me, look, don't say nothing. I make you the police chief here. Stay here. I said, go to hell. Sign my-- my pass. He wants me to stay in Blonie to-- to work for the Communists and become the police chief and everything. No. I went back to Krakow. And the guy--

speaker: interviewer
text: So wait. While you were in Blonie still, did you meet anybody else that you knew? Did you run into people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I met people and they told me-- I went to the post office. They told me a Galek was there. A Galek was there. I think my brother who survived, he was there. But anyway, I had one thing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. Did you go back into your house? Were you able To get anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes. People lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did they let you in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: But somebody else already occupied it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. But did they have any possession that you wanted or there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you pick up the pictures of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? Oh, I-- I it-- my-- my-- my cousin had it, who survived, some pictures that have it, a little snapshot. I had the snapshot, too. I don't know what happened. And-- and-- and my niece here made-- made-- made the copies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture of your-- your-- the wedding and the family, where did that-- who had that picture, your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my-- my cousin, yes, has the picture. And-- but anyway, I settled in Krakow. In Krakow, they didn't know I was a Jew. I speak Polish and Russian. And the-- and I lived in a section, [INAUDIBLE]. If you know Krakow, the-- the Polish kings are buried there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, they're preserved like Lenin was preserved. They preserved the Polish kings. And anyway, the-- the Polacks come over to me, you know the Jews came back from Russia? They must have a lot of money. Now let's go rob them and kill them. Oh my God. I said, what? Why-- why you want to rob them? How you know they got money? Anyway, and I was shaking. They don't know I was a Jew. I was shaking. Later on, I don't know [INAUDIBLE] in Moscow are the Russian army who surrendered the building and have an investigation what they did. And this happened at a police made the-- made the-- tried to organize the killing. The police. Tried to organize the killing of?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go in and rob the Jews and kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In order to get their money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But the Russian Army surrendered the building and American government in Germany gave the Polish government an order, you cannot control your people. Give the people a right to move and get out from there. Open the borders and let them go. And we got two weeks to pack and go-- and go. And the men who lived there were--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they have? They had announcements in the newspaper or did they have signs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No. There-- there-- there were-- the police got announcements. Anybody who wants to leave, the mayor of the city-- it's a beautiful city--

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful city, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow. Yes. And he said, you can leave but you have to leave everything what you have. Just take your anything. We-- we-- we crossed the border and we went by-- to a train to Czechoslovakia. In the train, we were not allowed to speak Russians. The Russian soldiers were there, the train, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what kind of papers were you traveling under?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had a-- a-- I had a-- a Polish birth certificate and a passport. I had a Krakow passport. And we landed in [? Austria-Germany. ?] And [? Austria-Germany, ?] we were there a day or two--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that was already 1945 then.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you ended up in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria we landed, in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria, we stayed there for a week and they put us--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say we, who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the camps in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. We, who did you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? With all the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which people? People from your building?

speaker: interviewee
text: People who-- who in the train-- with people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just people that were with you? No-- no person that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You Didn't know anybody with you? You met them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the [INAUDIBLE] camp. And we went to a camp in [INAUDIBLE], that's near Frankfurt. The-- the-- the German military [INAUDIBLE], a big place there. And they took over and they put us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Stuttgart-- in Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: This had to be after the war was over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. Because you were saying the end of '44. This has to be in '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '45, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '45. When-- when I was-- left Poland was in '45 and went to Austria and there they put us in-- in this camp. In this camp, I was volunteered to be a chief cook. I was their cook and we formed a soccer team.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A soccer team. And when I arrived to play soccer in Ulm, it's in-- near Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Stuttgart, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends were there who was with me in Poland, soccer players and everything. They said, you gonna stay there? You come with us. We settle you in a camp with the people who had the numbers. They called them [POLISH]. They were the [POLISH]. There was a camp, a beautiful camp [INAUDIBLE] they took all from the Germans. I said, no. You go back to Frankfurt. We go with you, take your [INAUDIBLE] and you settle here. And I settled there in Heidenheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Heidenheim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Heidenheim. This is between Ulm and Stuttgart, a beautiful city. And there I played soccer. The-- the owner supplied the food and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: The owner, that's United Nations Relief?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. United Nations supplied the food. Later on, they camped in Austria-- Australia. People, to-- to-- when they liquidated the camps, everybody-- the Catholics at the church guarantee it here in America with the jobs, this, they bring the Catholics, the Russians. And they-- and the HIAS took the Jews. And I said, I don't want to go to Australia, build in Australia. I want to go to America. And I came-- and I came, and the HIAS took me to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait, wait. It wasn't so simple. You didn't say one day, I'm gonna go to-- I wanna go--

speaker: interviewee
text: On, no. I-- I-- I stayed to the last.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- you signed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I-- I-- I stayed to the last. I didn't want to go no place if-- if the HIAS going to take me to America, I go. And later on, they-- they-- they had--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to register with them and you had to have a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I registered with them. And they had signatures here from the studio, the photographer where I worked. He signed that he has the job for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. OK. In other words, the HIAS found a place for you to work and he signed for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every factory that we went there, how many people you're gonna supply, how many people you're going to get. We're going to give them a home, but you give them a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So tell me first when you while-- when you were a soccer player, so you traveled all over--

speaker: interviewee
text: All over Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you became famous. People knew who you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were just playing with displaced people or you were playing with-- what league did you play in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I playing for every camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Ukraine, Polacks, every camp we played.

speaker: interviewer
text: You played against them all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Against, yeah. We played with the Polacks, we got orders, if you lose the game, you're not going to get the-- the food [? for ?] [? you ?] [? get ?] [? it. ?] We-- we just like a war when we played with them. But we won, one to nothing. Look, it's anything-- when I-- when I came to America, I worked very hard, day and night. I didn't speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which port you leave from?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left, which port did you leave from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left from Munich. So while you were in Europe, you didn't make any money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were playing soccer, no, you didn't make any money? All you did was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You played soccer and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- just for-- in Poland, I didn't get money, either. We played for glory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the city. We-- we-- here in America it's money.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but sometimes people pay you or sometimes you had a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a job on the side?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Here when I came to New York, the Vienna [INAUDIBLE]. You know [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE], that was a Jewish group.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they were the best soccer from Austria, in Vienna. And I know them, some people there. And I went up to them, I want to play in the team.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they say, we're going to pay you for a game. I said, no way. I want a job. I want a security and home. I have my father and mother. Here when I get injured, who's gonna pay for me? No, we're going to pay you for-- I said, forget about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they were going to pay you per game.

speaker: interviewee
text: By game.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it was no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year did you finally get your passage to-- to America? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '49, September of 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war had already finished four years and you didn't ever think about going to Israel at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. My brother went to Israel. He was in Germany, too, my-- my-- my brother. And the older brother, the HIAS took him here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you find your brothers at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after the war, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not anywhere. I find them in the war. I find them in Samarkand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. Right. That brother. But the other brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, the other was-- I never find him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You found two brothers survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two brothers were in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. They both were in Samarkand. I thought one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I lived with them together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So in 1949 you came to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel when you saw the Statue of Liberty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I was very, very excited. I went there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that day? What day was it, what date? Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. September-- September '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember the specific date?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The date, no. It was September. After three years. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I was a-- oh.
